---
title: "Streamlining the TFL Pipeline"
subtitle: "Solving high-friction rendering challenges with arframe"
author: ""
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    theme: default
    slide-number: true
    preview-links: auto
    view-distance: 10
    transition: fade
    code-copy: true
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
---

## 1. The Parallel QC Bottleneck {auto-animate=true}

The current stack obscures the final data structure, making `proc compare` difficult.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Current Stack (`tfrmt`)
Data is hidden inside nested, heavily customized ARD objects before GT rendering.

```r
# Heavy manual prep required
ard_tbl <- demog_ard |>
  mutate(
    TRT01A = unlist(...),
    label = unlist(...),
    stat = as.double(stat)
  ) |> ... # 150+ lines
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### `arframe`
Natively exports a clean, standard wide dataframe ready for validation.

```r
# direct conversion to wide
demog_wide <- fr_wide_ard(
  demog_ard,
  statistic = "{n} ({p}%)"
)

# Output is a simple dataframe
# Perfect for diffdf / proc compare
```
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
**Speaker Notes:**
- "Right now, `tfrmt` obscures the data structure. Parallel QC is a nightmare because you can't easily extract the wide format to compare against SAS."
- "With `arframe`, `fr_wide_ard()` natively generates a standard wide dataframe. Validation is literally just running `proc compare` or `diffdf`. No workarounds or hacking required."
:::

---

## 2. Standardization: Decimal Alignment {auto-animate=true}

Inconsistent formatting hacks lead to hours of debugging.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Current Stack
4 programmers write 4 different hacks (regex, padding, custom functions) to achieve decimal alignment in GT.

```r
# Example GT workaround
gt_table |> 
  cols_align_decimal(
    columns = everything()
  ) 
# Often fails on complex strings 
# or "(N=xx)" entries
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### `arframe`
One native, universally applied argument. 

```r
# Standardized across all tables
demog_spec <- demog_wide |>
  fr_cols(
    `Placebo` = fr_col(align = "decimal"),
    `Drug X`  = fr_col(align = "decimal")
  )
```
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
**Speaker Notes:**
- "If I ask four programmers to do decimal alignment in `tfrmt`, I get four completely different, hacky solutions. When one of them breaks, it takes hours to debug."
- "In `arframe`, decimal alignment is a native argument: `align = "decimal"`. Everyone does it the exact same way. It takes one second to read and zero seconds to debug."
:::

---

## 3. The Wide Table Problem {auto-animate=true}

Studies with 8+ treatment columns break standard rendering packages.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="100%"}
### Splitting columns across pages
`gt` and `tfrmt` require heavy custom chunking logic to span a single wide table (e.g., Stub + 4 cols on Page 1, Stub + remaining cols on Page 2).

### `arframe` implementation
Handled completely natively.

```r
vs_spec <- vs_long |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_cols(
    statistic = fr_col("Statistic", stub = TRUE),
    ... # 8 treatment arms
    .split = TRUE # Automatically paginates and repeats the stub
  )
```
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
**Speaker Notes:**
- "If you have a study with 8 treatment columns, `gt` and `tfrmt` simply break. They can't split columns across pages neatly."
- "You end up having to rewrite your own custom functions just to get it to print or split it into separate table views manually."
- "`arframe` handles this natively with `.split = TRUE`. It automatically paginates and carries your stub column over to the next page."
:::

---

## 4. Live Demo: Point-and-Click Generation {auto-animate=true}

Lowering the barrier to entry with **arbuilder**.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](https://i.imgur.com/your_screenshot_placeholder.png){width="100%" fig-align="center"}
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
<br><br>
- **No coding required:** Point-and-click UI
- **Standardized:** Select pre-built templates
- **Reproducible:** Automatically exports the exact backend R script.
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
**Speaker Notes:**
- "I know transitioning to new code can be daunting. So I built a UI layer on top called `arbuilder`. It’s a template generator."
- *[Stop talking here and open the Shiny app local instance]*
- *[Load data, click Demographics template, click export]*
- "Right now `arbuilder` works for demographics as a proof-of-concept. In the future, we can easily extend this to produce any figure, listing, or table format."
:::
